coredata.dc:description: Conductive agent can increase conductivity and construct pore structure in supercapacitor (SC) electrode as clean renewable energy. Carbon black (CB) is common conductive agent but its zero dimension hinders efficient charge-transfer conductive bridge. Combining CB with other carbon materials can possibly solve this issue. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) with high hydrophilicity and stable doping features is easier and cheaper to develop. Previous synthesis of GQD requires complex process and lacks large-scale production. In this study, novel one-step thermal treatment was provided to synthesize N-doped GQD. Citric acid and urea ratios were varied to synthesize N-doped GQD with different N-doping extents and electrical conductivities. CB and GQD mixtures, GQD and CB are used as conductive agent for fabricating SC electrodes. Activated carbon electrode with CB and GQD mixture shows the highest specific capacitance (CF) of 241.4 F/g at 20 mV/s, while electrodes without conductive agent and with CB conductive agent respectively shows CF of 41.5 and 108.6 F/g, due to more functional groups, defects, hydrophilic sites and electrochemical surface area of the former case. Symmetric SC with CB and GQD shows a maximum energy density of 10.54 Wh/kg at 550 W/kg, and CF retention of 85% and Coulombic efficiency of 80% after 5000 charging/discharging cycles.

head.ce:title: Novel synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dot as conductive agent for carbon based supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conductive agent can increase conductivity and construct pore structure in supercapacitor (SC) electrode as clean renewable energy. Carbon black (CB) is common conductive agent but its zero dimension hinders efficient charge-transfer conductive bridge. Combining CB with other carbon materials can possibly solve this issue. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) with high hydrophilicity and stable doping features is easier and cheaper to develop. Previous synthesis of GQD requires complex process and lacks large-scale production. In this study, novel one-step thermal treatment was provided to synthesize N-doped GQD. Citric acid and urea ratios were varied to synthesize N-doped GQD with different N-doping extents and electrical conductivities. CB and GQD mixtures, GQD and CB are used as conductive agent for fabricating SC electrodes. Activated carbon electrode with CB and GQD mixture shows the highest specific capacitance (CF) of 241.4 F/g at 20 mV/s, while electrodes without conductive agent and with CB conductive agent respectively shows CF of 41.5 and 108.6 F/g, due to more functional groups, defects, hydrophilic sites and electrochemical surface area of the former case. Symmetric SC with CB and GQD shows a maximum energy density of 10.54 Wh/kg at 550 W/kg, and CF retention of 85% and Coulombic efficiency of 80% after 5000 charging/discharging cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy generation and storage are of great importance in modern society [1–8]. Supercapacitor (SC) has attracted much attention as efficient energy storage device, due to its low-cost, high power density and long cycle life. SC stores charges using electric double layered capacitor (EDLC) and pseudocapacitor behaviors. The EDLC applies carbon material as active material, and stores charges using ion adsorption/desorption mechanism [9–11]. The fabrication of EDLC is less expensive since activated carbon (AC) can be synthesized using low-cost wastes. Also, the ion adsorption/desorption mechanism can induce better cycling stability and long-term usage [12–15].

The conductive paste is required to fabricate EDLC electrodes with carbon materials as the active material. Using the conductive paste to fabricate electrodes is advantageous on mass production for commercial application. The conductive paste is composed of an active material, a conductive agent, a binder and a solvent. The conductive agent is significant to influence the electrochemical performance of EDLC electrodes. The main function of the conductive agent is to enhance electrical conductivity. The pore structure and ionic conductivity can also be enhanced by designing efficient conductive agents [16,17]. Carbon black (CB) is the most common conductive agent for fabricating EDLC electrodes, due to its low-cost for suitable commercial applications, sphere-like structures for easy filling between active materials, and high conductivity for accelerating charge transportation [18–21]. However, the zero-dimension of CB may be unable to create conductive bridges for efficient charge transfer. Also, preferable configurations and higher conductivities are possible to achieve by designing other conductive agents. Numerous studies tried to synthesize new conductive agents such as reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [22–24], carbon nanotubes (CNT) [25,26] and graphene quantum dots (GQD) [27] to replace or combine with CB for further enhancing the electrochemical performance of SC. Zhang et al. prepared graphitized carbon onions by chemical vapor deposition using methane as the carbon source. The SC with carbon onions as the conductive agent shows better electrochemical performance than the SC with the commercial Super P®, due to the formation of nanopores among carbon onions for efficient electrolyte diffusion [23]. Yang et al. applied a microwave treatment on graphene to synthesize holey graphene mixed with CB as the conductive agent. The holey graphene can reduce migration distances between the electrode and the electrolyte, and hence the electrochemical performance of SC was enhanced [24]. Kim et al. synthesized sulfur-doped CNT by chemical vapor deposition as conducting agents for SC. The commercial Super P® and multi-walled CNT were also applied as the conductive agents for comparison. The SC with sulfur-doped CNT as the conductive agent shows better electrochemical performance due to higher conductivity and better pseudocapacitive behavior with S doping [26].

Among the conductive agents, GQD is easier to produce than rGO and CNT. Also, GQD has high hydrophilicity and stable doping features. The zero dimension of GQD is suitable for combining with other carbon materials to design various conductive agents. GQD was synthesized using complex methods in previous studies. Gu et al. fabricated N-doped GQD through infrared carbonization of citric acid and urea [28]. Sangabathula and co-workers synthesized GQD using hydrothermal process with urea and citric acid as precursors [29]. Tan et al. synthesized N-doped GQD via a three-step fabrication process including microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, carbonization and KOH activation with citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source [30]. To avoid complex synthesis and achieve large-scale production for fitting commercial requirements of conductive pastes, novel methods are needed to develop for synthesizing the conductive agent of GQD more efficiently.

In this work, a novel one-step thermal treatment was proposed to synthesize N-doped GQD as the conductive agent of SC. Different ratios of citric acid and urea were used to synthesize N-doped GQD with various N-doping levels and conductivities. The self-made GQD was then mixed with CB as the conductive agent. The AC-based electrodes without the conductive agent and those with pure CB and CB/GQD as the conductive agent show specific capacitance (CF) of 41.5, 108.6, and 241.4 F/g at 20 mV/s, respectively. The better energy storage ability of the AC-based electrode with CB/GQD as the conductive agent is due to numerous functional groups, more defects, more hydrophilic sites and larger electrochemical surface area. The symmetric SC (SSC) shows a maximum energy density of 10.54 Wh/kg at power density of 550 W/kg. The CF retention of 85% and Coulombic efficiency of around 80% after 5000 times charging/discharging process were also achieved for the SSC.

Nitrogen-doped GQD was synthesized by a one-step thermal treatment. Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99%, Echo Chemical Co. Ltd.) and urea (CH4N2O, 99%, Echo Chemical Co. Ltd.) were mixed by weight ratios of 1:0.3, 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 in a high-energy ball mill at 500 rpm using Zr balls (Tosoh Co.) for 1 min. Subsequently, the mixture was spread evenly on thermally resistive glass pan, which was then put in a tubular furnace to carry out a sintering process with three steps. The first step is to ramp temperature from ambient temperature to 200 °C for 30 min, the second step is to maintain the temperature at 200 °C for 20 min, and the last step is to natural cool the mixture from 200 °C to ambient temperature. After the sintering process, all mixtures were ball-milled and sieved through a stainless foil mesh (300 mesh). The GQD synthesized using citric acid and urea with weight ratios of 1:0.3, 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 was nominated as C1U03, C1O05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3, respectively. The yield of GQD is around 5 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of conductive paste and supercapacitor electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The conductive paste was prepared by mixing graphene (XG Sciences Inc.), nitrogen doped GQD, carbon black (CB) and polyvinylidene difluoride (PVdF, 100%, Dinhaw Enterprise Co. LTD.) with different weight percentages in dimethylformamide (DMF, Fisher UK). Table S1 in supporting information (SI) shows the recipe of the conductive paste containing pure graphene (none) and those with conductive agents. The conductive paste comprising the conductive agent of CB and GQD with mass ratio of 8:2, 6:4, 4:6 and 2:8 was nominated as C8G2, C6G4, C4G6 and C2G8, respectively. The mixture was stirred for 2 h and then ultrasonicated for 30 min. Carbon cloth (CC) was used as the conductive substrate of supercapacitor electrode. The CC was cleaned in sequence using acetone and alcohol in ultrasonication for 30 min and dried at 60 °C overnight [31]. The conductive paste was dispersed on CC using a doctor blade method and dried at 60 °C overnight. The supercapacitor electrode was then obtained. The have broader application, AC (Echo Chemical Co. Ltd.) was also used as the active material to replace graphene. The recipes of the AC-based paste containing pure AC and those with the conductive agent were shown in Table S2 in SI. The SSC was assembled using two optimized carbon-based electrodes and the electrolyte of 1 M H2SO4.

The morphology of carbon materials was observed using the field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Nova NanoSEM 230, FEI, Oregon, USA) and field emission gun transmission microscope (FEG-TEM, Philips Tecnai F30). Composition of carbon materials was examined via the X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, X'Pert3 Powder, PANalytical), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG Scientific ESCALAB 250) measurement with Al Ka radiation, Raman spectrometer (Dongwoo, DM500i) with the green laser excitation (514.5 nm), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, PerkinElmer, Spectrum Two) spectrum. Electrochemical performances of carbon-based electrodes were measured in a three-electrode system containing Pt counter electrode and Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The cyclic voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GC/D) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out using the potentiostat/galvanostat instrument equipped with an FRA2 module (PGSTAT 204, Autolab, Eco–Chemie, the Netherlands). The EIS measurement was conducted at open-circuit potential and frequency ranges from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz.

The morphology of self-made GQD was observed using SEM images. The self-made GQD was synthesized using citric acid and urea, whose SEM images were respectively shown in Fig. 1
(a) and (b) as references. The citric acid shows bulk aggregations, while urea presents separated particles with much smaller sizes. The self-made GQD was synthesized using different citric acid to urea ratios. Fig. 1(c)-(g) respectively shows the SEM images of C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3. The aggregations and bulk structures were formed. The C1U03 composed of largest ratio of citric acid present bulk aggregations with limited particles dispersed. With the urea increasing, the resulting GQD shows less bulk structure but more separated particles uniformly distributed on bulk. The inserted figure of Fig. 1(e) shows enlarged SEM image of C1U1. The TEM image of C1U1 was also inserted in Fig. 1(e). Some particle size is less than 10 nm, indicating its quantum dot feature. The larger particle size may be due to the aggregation of QD. The functions of citric acid and urea are the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. Based on the same formation extent of carbon, doping more nitrogen can reduce the particle size of QD.

Furthermore, the XRD patterns were used to examine the composition of GQD prepared using different citric acid and urea ratios, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The standard patterns of cyanuric acid (JCPDS #23–1637) [28] and graphite (JCPDS #75–2078) [32,33] were also shown in this figure for comparison. A strong peak at 2 theta values of 26.20°, 26.35°, 26.87°, 26.87° and 27.16° was obtained in the patterns of C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3, respectively. This main peak for GQD is corresponding to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon. The shift of diffraction angle (002) toward higher degrees was observed in the patterns for samples prepared using more urea. The peak shift is due to the changes in stoichiometric composition by doping nitrogen. More urea incorporated in synthesizing GQD may induce higher nitrogen doping extents, and hence to more shifts of diffraction angle (002) toward higher degrees. This phenomenon is consistent with the reduced particle sizes of GQD prepared using more urea, as observed in SEM images. The d-spacing of GQD is ranged from 0.335 to 0.340 nm, which is close to the d-spacing of graphite. Also, this main peak split into two peaks in the pattern of C1U3, owing to the overloaded nitrogen-contained functional groups for forming different layered structures. On the other hand, the patterns of C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 show extra peaks corresponding to cyanuric acid, which was formed after calcination of urea. The peaks corresponding to cyanuric acid become more obvious in the patterns of GQD prepared using more urea, suggesting the incomplete reaction between citric acid and urea and the overloaded reactant of urea. However, this phenomenon is only serious for C1U3. The patterns of C1U1 and C1U2 merely present slight peaks at 19.71° and 29.65° corresponding to (111) and (202) facets of cyanuric acid.

The surface functional groups were examined using FTIR spectra, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The weak OH bonding at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the physical adsorption of water. The peaks at 3100–3500 cm−1, 1570 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 are resulted from NH stretching vibration, NH bending vibration and CN bending vibration, respectively. These peaks are the main features of the ammonia group. The hydrophobic functional groups of CH bonding (2850 and 2920 cm−1) and CO bonding (1660 cm−1), and the hydrophilic functional groups of CC bonding (11,731 cm−1) and CO bonding (1051 cm−1) were observed for all the samples [34]. The peak intensities of ammonia group and hydrophilic groups increase in the spectra of GQD prepared using more urea. This phenomenon is preferable for the active material operated in aqueous solutions. However, the peak intensities of hydrophobic groups enhanced for GQD prepared using more urea, too. Therefore, the GQD prepared using a moderate amount of urea may display suitable ammonia, hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups to achieve better electrochemical performance. The defect and graphite natures were further analyzed using Raman spectra [35], as shown in Fig. 2(c). The intensity ratio of defect to graphite (ID/IG) values of C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 are 1.12, 1.05, 1.05, 1.65 and 1.89, respectively. The ID/IG value increases largely for GQD prepared with higher urea ratios, i.e., C1U2 and C1U3, owing to the larger nitrogen doping amounts to create abundant defects in graphite.

The GQD was possibly formed by following the mechanisms occurring in the hydrothermal process. The first step is the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea, which has melting points at 156 and 135 °C, respectively. Thermal pyrolysis of carbon precursors can produce large amounts of free radicals like CH3·, OH· and NH2· at their decomposition temperatures. The second step is the liquid-phase growth of GQD and sequent N-doping and O-decorating. The carbon atoms begin to form spherical GQD in liquid phase. At the same time, in-situ N- and O-decorating or N-doping were proceeded. The CN, CO, CN and CO bonds in as-stacked carbon architecture were then generated. Also, GQD was possibly formed in two different pathways regarding the contents of urea precursor. With larger amounts of urea incorporating in synthesis, N-doped GQD and cyanuric acid could form. With smaller amounts of urea incorporating in synthesis, the relatively higher citric acid content could induce formation of N-doped GQDs composed of pyridinic/pyrrolic/graphitic N groups.

As the conductive agent, the conductivity is of the great importance. The conductivity of GQD was measured using the 4-points probe, and the relation of conductivity to sorts of GQD was shown in Fig. 2(d). The C1U03 and C1U05 show conductivities less than 5 S/mm, while C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 show much higher conductivities of 25.41, 20.43 and 17.11 S/mm, respectively. The low conductivities of C1U03 and C1U05 are due to the small nitrogen doping levels. The C1U1 shows the highest conductivity, and the conductivity decreases for GQD prepared using more urea (C1U2 and C1U3). Although nitrogen doping can enhance the conductivity of the carbon material, too much nitrogen doped in graphite could seriously destroy the carbon connections and create too many defects. As a result, conductivity could be largely improved by doping moderate amounts of nitrogen to develop suitable defects without destructing the basic configuration of graphite. It is worthy to compare the results of conductivity measurement and Raman spectra. It is inferred that IG value is proportional to conductivity. The C1U03 and C1U05 show small conductivities and small ID/IG values, which may be due to the poor graphite nature and less defect formation. The C1U1 shows a large conductivity but a small ID/IG value. This unique phenomenon may be caused by strong graphite features and suitable defect amounts. The C1U2 and C1U3 show large conductivities and large ID/IG values, which may be attributed to the strong graphite characteristics and large numbers of defects due to the higher nitrogen doping amounts.

The detailed compositions were analyzed using XPS spectra. Fig. 3(a) shows the survey spectra of C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3. All spectra show clear N1s, C1s and O1s signals, suggesting the successful synthesis of N-doped carbon. The N1s spectra of C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 were presented Fig. 3(b). All GQD shows pyridinic forms of nitrogen. The nitrogen to carbon ratio (N/C) of 0.44, 0.31, 0.26, 0.22 and 0.18 was respectively obtained for C1U3, C1U2, C1U1, C1U05 and C1U03 in the corresponding figure. The higher amounts of urea participated in synthesis lead to higher contents of nitrogen. This result suggests that the nitrogen amount can be well controlled by varying the urea concentration. The C1s spectrum of C1U3, C1U2, C1U1, C1U05 and C1U03 were presented Fig. 3(c)-(g), respectively. The CC, CO and CO bounds were observed in spectra of all GQD synthesized using different ratios of precursors. Moreover, the C1U3 synthesized using the highest ratio of urea showed an additional peak corresponding to COOH. On the other hand, the O1s spectrum of C1U3, C1U2, C1U1, C1U05 and C1U03 were presented Fig. 3(h)-(l), respectively. The characteristic peaks of quinone and O=C-OH were obtained in all spectra. It is found that the quinone amount is higher than O=C-OH in GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of self-made GQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical performance of self-made GQD electrodes was analyzed using CV, GC/D and Nyquist plots. Fig. 4(a) shows the CV curves at 10 mV/s for self-made GQD electrodes prepared using different citric acid and urea ratios. The CV curve of the sample prepared without adding urea (C1U0) was also shown in this figure for comparison. The CV curves of all electrodes show similar rectangular shapes with larger current densities at high potential regions. The integrating area of C1U0 is smaller than those for other self-made GQD electrodes, indicating the benefits of adding urea for enhancing the capacitive property. A smaller oxidation peak was observed at 0.56 VAg/AgCl in the CV curves of C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 electrodes. The carbon nature of GQD was displayed in the rectangular shape of CV curves, and the small oxidation peak suggests the functional groups on the surface of GQD. Also, the suddenly increased current at the high potential region and decreased current at the low potential region were observed in the CV curves of all electrodes, due to high resistances in the electrodes which merely contained the conductive agent. The CF value was calculated from CV curves using Eq.
(1) as follows [36].
(1)
C F
=
∫ IdV
∆ V ∙ υ
where ∫IdV is the integrated area of CV curve, ∆V is the potential window (V), and υ is the scan rate for measuring the CV curve. The CF values of GQD electrodes were listed in Table 1 . All electrodes show small CF values, due to the lack of the active material. The C1U1 electrode shows the largest CF value of 0.87 F/g, due to the suitable particle size, abundant defects, moderate N-doping level and high conductivity. The smaller CF values of C1U03 and C1U05 electrodes are due to the aggregated structures, less N-doping amounts and low conductivities. The reduced electrochemical performances of C1U2 and C1U3 electrodes are caused by the excess nitrogen doping amount to form cyanuric acid with poor electroactivities and conductivities. On the other hand, the GC/D plot at 0.1 A/g for self-made GQD electrodes prepared using different citric acid and urea ratios was shown in Fig. 4(b). The discharge time is limited for all electrodes, due to the lack of the active material. The CF value was calculated from GC/D curves using Eq.
(2) as follows [37].
(2)
C F
=
I ∙ t
∆ V
where I is the current density for measuring GC/D curve, t is the discharging time in the GC/D curve, and ∆V is the potential window (V). The CF values of GQD electrodes were also listed in Table 1 for comparison. The C1U1 electrode again shows the highest CF value, and the trend of CF value is consistent as obtained from CV curves. Furthermore, the resistance in the electrode was examined using the Nyquist plot, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The equivalent circuit was inserted in this figure for fitting the resistances. The series resistance (RS) represents the total resistance in the electrode, including the material and interfacial resistances. The charge-transfer resistance (RCT) is the obstacles at interface between electrode and electrolyte [38]. The RS and RCT values can be obtained from the x-intercept and the diameter of the fitted semicircle at the high frequency region, respectively [39,40]. The RS and RCT values of GQD electrodes were listed in Table 1. All electrodes show similar RS values ranged from 1.35 to 1.49 Ω. These small values suggest the excellent conductive agent feature of self-made GQD. The RCT values are much larger for C1U03 and C1U05 electrodes. This phenomenon is caused by the bulk aggregation of C1U03 and C1U05 electrodes without close contacts with the substrate and less nitrogen doping levels with poor conductivities. The C1U1 electrode shows the smallest RCT value, due to the moderate nitrogen doping amount for inducing high conductivity and abundant defects. The C1U2 and C1U3 electrodes show higher RCT values than that of C1U1, owing to the excess nitrogen doping amount with too many defects and serious destructions of graphite. Furthermore, surface area plays important roles on electrolyte contacts and hence electrochemical performances. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of electrodes was then analyzed by measuring CV curves at different scan rates [41]. Fig. S1(a)-(e) in SI respectively shows the CV curves at different scan rates for C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 electrodes. Fig. 4(d) shows the relation between current differences at 0.4 VAg/AgCl and scan rates for C1U03, C1U05, C1U1, C1U2 and C1U3 electrodes. The slope of the fitted line is proportional to ECSA of electrodes. The ECSA increases for GQD electrodes prepared using more urea, and the largest ECSA was achieved for the C1U1 electrode. Further increasing the urea amounts to synthesize GQD electrodes leads to the reduction of ECSA. The smaller ECSA for the GQD electrode prepared using less urea is due to the formation of bulk aggregation structures. The reduction of ECSA for C1U2 and C1U3 electrodes comparing to that of the C1U1 electrode is owing to the higher coverages of active sites by cyanuric acid for the former cases.

In all, the C1U1 electrode shows the best electrochemical performance due to its excellent physical properties. Therefore, C1U1 was applied as the conductive agent in the following text to mix with the active material for fabricating SC electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of graphene-based and AC-based electrodes with CB, GQD, and CB/GQD composites as the conductive agent
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimized conductive agent, C1U1, the SC electrodes were fabricated using graphene and AC as the active material. The electrochemical performance of SC electrodes using graphene as the active material was firstly analyzed. The conductive agents of pure CB, pure GQD (C1U1), and the mixtures of CB and GQD were added in graphene-based electrodes. Fig. 5(a) shows the CV curves of graphene-based electrodes with different conductive agents. The CV curves are not perfect rectangular but present one obvious couple of redox peaks, since there are several functional groups attached on graphene. The graphene-based electrodes with CB and CB/GQD mixtures as conductive agents present one couple of redox peaks at similar potential regions (0.6 to 0.7 VAg/AgCl), while the graphene-based electrode with the pure GQD as the conductive agent shows one couple of redox peaks at smaller potential regions (0.4 to 0.5 VAg/AgCl). This phenomenon indicates the dominated role of CB on conductive properties of graphene-based electrodes than GQD. The CF values of graphene-based electrodes composed of different conductive agents were listed in Table 2 . The CF value increases for the graphene-based electrodes with more GQD as the conductive agent. The graphene-based electrodes with C6G4, C4G6 and C2G8 as the conductive agents show larger CF values than the graphene-based electrode with the pure GQD as the conductive agent. This phenomenon indicates the necessary addition of CB in GQD as the conductive agent. It is inferred that CB with much smaller sizes can fill in pores between GQD smoothly and further enhance the conductivity. As a result, the graphene-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent presents the largest CF value of 24.5 F/g. In addition, the graphene-based electrode with C2G8 as conductive agent shows reduction of the CF value (14.6 F/g). This result is caused by the insufficient CB acting as connecting bridges between GQD which possesses larger sizes and poorer connections. On the other hand, the electrochemical performance of graphene-based electrodes with pure CB and pure GQD was firstly compared. The graphene-based electrode with pure CB as conductive agent shows a smaller CF value of 8.1 F/g than the graphene-based electrode with pure GQD as conductive agent (11.9 F/g). This result is mainly due to the higher ECSA of GQD than that of CB, as evidenced in Fig. 5(b) which shows the relation between current difference at 0.4 VAg/AgCl and scan rates. The slope of the fitted line is proportional to ECSA. The graphene electrode without conductive agent shows much smaller slope than those of other electrodes. This result strongly indicates the significance of conductive agent on enhancing ECSA and electrochemical performance of graphene-based electrode. The slopes of 96.2 and 162.4 mF/cm2 were respectively obtained for the graphene-based electrode with pure CB and pure GQD as conductive agent. The ECSA of the graphene-based electrode with pure GQD is 1.7-fold of the ECSA for the graphene-based electrode with pure CB. This result strongly supports the function of adding GQD in carbon black to improve the electrochemical performances of graphene-based supercapacitors. The higher ECSA of GQD can induce more redox reactions and provide more active sites for ion adsorption/desorption. Also, the numerous functional groups on GQD can generate abundant redox reactions for better energy storge, as confirmed by the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2(b)). In addition, the electrodes with mixed conductive agents show larger ECSA than those with pure CB and pure GQD as conductive agent. This phenomenon is caused by the reduction on aggregated structure of GQD via filling CB in-between. The graphene electrode with C4G6 as conductive agent shows the largest ECSA, which favors for generating abundant redox reactions and improving energy storage ability.

The GC/D curves of graphene-based electrodes with different conductive agents were then presented in Fig. 5(c), and their CF values were also listed in Table 2 for comparison. The GC/D curves of all electrodes are highly symmetric, suggesting the excellent charge/discharge reversibility. One couple of plateaus in charging and discharging curves was observed for all electrodes, which is consistent with their CV curves with one couple of redox peaks. The trend of CF value obtained from GC/D curves is the same as those observed in CV curves. The electrode with the pure CB as the conductive agent shows the smallest CF value, and the electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent presents the largest CF value of 13.5 F/g. In addition, the resistances in the graphene electrodes with different conductive agents were analyzed using Nyquist plot, as shown in Fig. 5(d). The corresponding RS and RCT values were listed in Table 2. The electrode with the pure CB as the conductive agent shows the largest RS value, indicating the smaller conductivity of CB than GQD-based conductive agents. The electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent shows the smallest RS value, owing to the suitable CB to GQD ratio with high CB connections between GQD and the sufficient GQD to display excellent conductive properties. The small RCT values are similar for all electrodes. This phenomenon is attributed to the small resistance between graphene and the electrolyte. Again, the ECSA of graphene-based electrodes with different conductive agents was examined. Fig. S2(a)-(g) in SI respectively shows the CV curves at different scan rates for graphene electrodes without conductive agent and with conductive agents of CB, C8G2, C6G4, C4G6, C2G8 and GQD, respectively. Furthermore, the high-rate charge/discharge ability is also important for an excellent energy storage device. Therefore, the relation of CF value to the scan rates for measuring the CV curves and to the current densities for measuring the GC/D curves were examined, as respectively shown in Fig. 5
(e) and (f). The CV curves at different scan rates and GC/D curves at different current densities for graphene electrodes without conductive agent and with conductive agents of CB, C8G2, C6G4, C4G6, C2G8 and GQD were presented in Figs. S3 and S4 in SI, respectively. All electrodes show high CF retentions at different measured conditions. The electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent shows the CF retentions of 99%, 88%, 86% and 83% respectively at 2-, 3-, 4-, and 5-folds enhancements on scan rates (Fig. 5(e)). Also, the CF retentions of 95%, 88%, 83% and 83% were respectively achieved for the electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent at 2-, 4-, 6-, and 8-folds enhancements on current densities (Fig. 5(f)). The high retention of CF values for the electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent again supports its excellent electrochemical performance.

To emphasize the contribution of GQD as conductive agent and broaden its applications, the active material of AC was also used to fabricate SC electrodes. Before discussing the AC electrodes, clear comparisons of electrochemical performance of graphene electrodes were provided. The CV curves, GC/D curves, and Nyquist plot of graphene electrodes without the conductive agent and with CB and C4G6 as the conductive agent were respectively shown in Fig. 6(a)-(c). The CF values and resistance values were listed in Table 3 for comparison. The redox potentials in CV curves and GC/D curves are similar for all electrodes, suggesting that the main redox reaction is contributed from the active material of graphene. The much larger integrated area and discharge time were obtained for the graphene-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent, indicating the excellent electrocapacitive activity of C4G6. Also, the graphene-based electrode without the conductive agent shows a much larger RCT value than those of the graphene-based electrodes with CB and C4G6 as the conductive agent. This result suggests that CB and C4G6 cannot only act as the conductive agent to improve conductivity but also play as the connected bridge to reduce the interfacial resistance between the electrode and the electrolyte.

The similar analysis was also applied on AC-based electrodes. The CV curves, GC/D curves and Nyquist plot of AC-based electrodes without the conductive agent and with the conductive agent of CB and C4G6 were provided in Fig. 6(d)-(f), respectively. The CF values and resistance values were listed in Table 3 for comparison. The obvious rectangular shape with almost no redox peaks were observed in the CV curves for all AC-based electrodes. This phenomenon again suggests that the main contribution of redox behavior is from the active material but not from the conductive agent. However, the conductive agent can still influence the redox behavior of the active material such as redox potential shifts in some extents. The GC/D curves show highly symmetric properties for all AC-based electrode, indicating the high charge/discharge reversibility of AC. Again, the much larger integrated area of CV curves and discharging time of GC/D curves were obtained for the AC-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent. The largest CF value of 241.4 F/g at 10 mV/s was successfully obtained for the AC-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent. These results prove the excellent electrochemical performance of the AC-based electrode using C4G6 as the conductive agent. Also, the AC-based electrode without the conductive agent shows larger RS and RCT values, again indicating the excellent electrochemical properties of CB and C4G6 to enhance the conductivity and reduce the interfacial resistance of electrodes. The ECSA of AC-based electrodes with and without conductive agents was also examined. Fig. S5(a)-(c) in SI respectively shows the CV curves at different scan rates for AC-based electrodes without conductive agent and with CB and C4G6 as the conductive agent. The relation between the current difference and the scan rate was shown in Fig. 6(g). The slope of fitted lines was also listed in this figure. The AC-based electrode without conductive agent shows a smaller ECSA than those of the AC-based electrodes with conductive agents. Also, the AC-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent shows the largest ECSA. This result is consistent with that obtained for the graphene-based electrode. The high-rate charge/discharge performance of AC-based electrodes was also examined using CV curves at different scan rates and GC/D curves at different current densities. Fig. S5(d)-(f) in SI respectively shows the CV curves at different scan rates for AC-based electrodes without the conductive agent and with CB and C4G6 as conductive agents. Fig. S5(g)-(i) in SI respectively shows the GC/D curves at different current densities for AC-based electrodes without the conductive agent and with CB and C4G6 as conductive agents. The relations between the CF value to the scan rate and to the current density were shown in Fig. 6
(h) and (i), respectively. The CF retentions of the AC-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent were indicated in these figures. With 5-fold enhancements on the scan rate and the current density, the CF retentions of 57% and 72% were respectively obtained for the AC-based electrode with C4G6 as the conductive agent. Furthermore, it is worthy to compare the electrochemical performance improvements between the AC-based and graphene-based electrodes with and without conductive agents. The enhancement of electrochemical performance for the graphene-based electrode with conductive agent is much larger than that for the AC-based electrode with conductive agent. This phenomenon may be due to the much poorer electrochemical performance of the graphene-based electrode than the AC-based electrode. Due to the worse physical properties such as small surface area and poor pore structure of graphene, the incorporation of conductive agents can more efficiently compensate for these weaknesses. However, the AC-based electrode already has better structure than graphene, adding conductive agent in AC cannot improve the electrochemical performance as much as in graphene. The porous structure is quite important for charge storage in electrochemical systems, since electrolyte ions diffuse and migrate into active sites of active materials through pores. The extents for adsorption of ions and generation of redox reaction are proportional to the porosity of electrodes [40–43]. On the other hand, the poor properties presented in Figs. 4 and 5 are owing to the poor performance of the commercial graphene not the conducting agent of GQD proposed in the present work. By using the commercial activated carbon as the active material, much better electrochemical properties were successfully achieved as shown in Fig. 6(d)-(i). The CF value of 241.4 F/g for the AC + C4G6 electrode is comparable to those of the similar systems reported in previous works.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of SSC with AC electrodes prepared using optimized CB and GQD composites as conductive agent
ce:section.ce:para:
An SSC was then assembled using AC-based electrodes with C4G6 as the conductive agent for wider applications. Fig. 7(a) shows CV curves at 10 mV/s in different potential windows. All CV curves present rectangular shapes due to the carbon nature. Obvious water redox reactions happened at potentials higher than 1.2 V. Therefore, the suitable potential window of 1.1 V was decided for this SSC. With the potential window of 1.1 V, the CV curves were then measured at different scan rates, as shown in Fig. 7(b). The CV curves maintained similar shapes at all scan rates without obvious redox peaks. This phenomenon suggests the high charge/discharge reversibility for this SSC. The GC/D curves were also measured using different potential windows (Fig. 7(c)). The high symmetry features were observed for all GC/D curves. The IR drop is relatively large in the curve with the potential window of 1.2 V. Hence, the suitable potential window of 1.1 V was again determined. Fig. 7(d) presents the GC/D curves at different current densities in the potential window of 1.1 V. All GC/D curves show very high symmetry, again indicating the high charge/discharge reversibility for this SSC. Based on Fig. 7(d), the Ragone plot was plotted to investigate energy and power densities, as presented in Fig. 7(e). The equations for calculating energy density (E) and power density (P) were shown in Eqs.
(3) and (4) as follows [44].
(3)
E =
1 2
C F
∆
V 2
(4)
P =
E t
where ∆V (V) is potential window and t (s) is discharge time. The largest energy density of 10.54 Wh/kg was obtained at power density of 550 W/kg, while at the largest power density of 2750.7 W/kg the energy density remained 7.07 Wh/kg. Last but not the least, the long-term cycling stability of this SSC was evaluated by measuring the GC/D curves repeatedly for 5000 times. The relation between CF retention and Coulombic efficiency to the cycle number was shown in Fig. 7(f). The CF retention of 85% was obtained after 5000 times charge/discharge process. The Coulombic efficiency of around 80% was achieved during the whole charge/discharge process. The decrease on the Coulombic efficiency may be due to the much longer charging time than discharging time. In the charging process, electrons are stored by two ways, including statistic electrical accumulation and redox reactions. In the discharging process, electrons stored by statistic electrical accumulation may not be totally released and partial irreversible reactions may occur for redox reactions. These phenomena may become more serious when longer cycles were applied for testing the stability.

Furthermore, the contribution of EDLC and pseudo-capacitance were further analyzed using Trasatti method [45]. The relation of capacitance to scan rate-0.5 and of capacitance0.5 to scan rate0.5 were plotted in Fig. 8
(a) and (b) , respectively. Based on Cottrell equation, the y-intercept of the fitted line in Fig. 8
(a) and (b) is respectively related to EDLC contribution and inverse of total capacitance contribution. Therefore, the contribution from EDLC and pseudo-capacitance was calculated to be 15.3% and 74.7%, respectively for the SSC with AC + C4G6 electrodes.

Nitrogen-doped graphite quantum dots were firstly synthesized using the one-step thermal treatment as the conductive agent of SC electrodes. Different ratios of citric acid and urea were used to synthesize GQD. The optimized GQD shows abundant functional groups and defects as well as high ECSA and conductivity. The GQD was further mixed with CB as the conductive agent. The CB with small sizes acted as connected bridges to improve contacts between GQD. The AC-based electrode with the optimized ratio of CB and GQD as the conductive agent presented the highest CF value of 241.4 F/g at 20 mV/s. An SSC composed of AC-based electrodes with the optimized CB/GQD mixtures as the conductive agent presented a maximum energy density of 10.54 Wh/kg at 550 W/kg. The CF retention of 85% and Coulombic efficiency of around 80% after 5000 charging/discharge cycles were also obtained. The combination of CB and GQD is of great importance to determine the conductivity of electrodes. In the future, it is suggested to synthesize CB and GQD composites prior to incorporating in the conductive paste. Designing efficient CB/GQD composites in the first step and then adding this conductive agent in the conductive paste may be more helpful than separately adding CB and GQD in the conductive paste to fabricate SC electrodes.
